articles.title: What Is Red Sphere Finance, the CBZ Arm Now Powering ZEEX Receivables?
articles.summary: Red Sphere Microfinance has just been named the anchor financier of the Zimbabwe Stock Exchange's new trade-receivables platform on ZEEX. It is a wholly owned CBZ subsidiary, a credit-only microfinance institution licensed since 2019. Here is what it is, what it lends, and what its new role means.

articles.title: Zimbabwe Moves to Regulate Crypto: Mandatory Registration, $500 Fees and FIU Oversight
articles.summary: Crypto firms in Zimbabwe must now register each year with the Financial Intelligence Unit and pay a $500 fee, or face criminal charges. The country's first comprehensive virtual-asset framework, and a shift from the 2018 banking ban toward formal oversight.

articles.title: The Tech Stack Will Decide ZEEX. The ZSE Has Been Quietly Building Toward It
articles.summary: Whether Zimbabwe's new SME exchange works will come down to the technology it runs on. The ZSE has a decade-long modernisation record and a brand-new trading system finished months ago. The open question is what, exactly, ZEEX will be built on.

articles.title: INVESCI Backs ZEEX: Zimbabwe's New SME Exchange Lands Its First Institutional Partner
articles.summary: Days after winning approval, the Zimbabwe Stock Exchange has signed INVESCI, the country's top-rated asset manager, as the first institutional partner for ZEEX. The most encouraging signal yet for a venture that still has plenty to prove.

articles.title: Exclusive: Air Zimbabwe Rebuilds Its Booking Engine Before London, in Silence
articles.summary: Days before its London return, Air Zimbabwe has switched on a new reservation and online booking system. It is real preparation, yet the news reached the public through a stock-market filing and a foreign partner, not its passengers, while the website sits on a maintenance page.

articles.title: Quiet Money, Deep Roots: How the Oak Foundation Backs Zimbabwe's Communities
articles.summary: The Geneva-based Oak Foundation has funded Zimbabwean causes since 1983. In 2025 its Zimbabwe programme made USD 7.9 million in grants, backing palliative care, landmine clearance, regenerative farming and care for children who have lost parents.
